---
title: "Technical Appendix: Abstract-to-Publication Matching Pipeline"
subtitle: "Supplement to: Publication Rate of Oral Presentations at the AAGL Global Congress, 2012-2023"
author: "Tyler Muffly, MD"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output: word_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(readr); library(dplyr); library(here); library(knitr)

d <- read_csv(here("output", "abstracts_with_matches.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
strat <- read_csv(here("output", "aim4_strategy_performance.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
val <- read_csv(here("output", "validation_metrics.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
s <- read_csv(here("output", "sensitivity_analyses.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
cox <- read_csv(here("output", "aim2b_cox_regression.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
ph <- read_csv(here("output", "cox_ph_assumption.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

cfg <- yaml::read_yaml(here("config.yml"))

n_total <- nrow(d)
n_congresses <- length(unique(d$congress_year))
yr_min <- min(d$congress_year)
yr_max <- max(d$congress_year)
n_definite <- sum(d$classification == "definite")
n_probable <- sum(d$classification == "probable")

xml_cache <- length(list.files(here("data", "cache", "pubmed_xml")))
html_cache <- length(list.files(here("data", "cache", "sd_html")))
```

## A1. Pipeline Architecture

The abstract-to-publication matching pipeline is implemented as a modular, fully reproducible R codebase comprising 20 R scripts, 12 test files (`r 391` unit and semantic tests), and a Shiny web application for human adjudication. The pipeline executes in 10 sequential steps, with checkpoint-based resume support for API-dependent stages. All source code and deidentified output data are available at https://github.com/mufflyt/abstract_lifetime.

### A1.1 System Requirements

- R version `r paste(R.version$major, R.version$minor, sep = ".")`
- 22 R packages (tidyverse core, rentrez, xml2, httr, jsonlite, survival, gender, shiny, bslib, googlesheets4)
- Disk cache: `r xml_cache` PubMed XML files, `r html_cache` ScienceDirect HTML files
- External APIs: NCBI E-Utilities (PubMed), CrossRef, Europe PMC, OpenAlex, Semantic Scholar, genderize.io
- Runtime: approximately 3-4 hours for a full pipeline execution on `r n_total` abstracts

### A1.2 Pipeline Steps

| Step | Script | Description | Checkpoint |
|------|--------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | `01b_parse_web.R` | Scrape JMIG supplement TOC + individual article pages from ScienceDirect | HTML cache |
| 1d | `01d_tag_session_type.R` | Walk TOC DOM to assign Oral/Video labels per abstract | - |
| 2 | `02_clean_abstracts.R` | Normalize titles/authors, extract 20+ predictor variables via NLP | - |
| 3 | `03_search_pubmed.R` | 6-strategy PubMed search per abstract via NCBI E-Utilities | RDS |
| 3b | `03b_search_crossref.R` | Supplementary search: CrossRef + Europe PMC + OpenAlex + Semantic Scholar | RDS per source |
| 3c | `03c_doi_chain_search.R` | Reverse citation search via OpenAlex (DOI-based) | RDS |
| 4 | `04_score_matches.R` | Composite scoring of all candidate publications | - |
| 5 | `05_adjudicate.R` | Apply Cochrane-aligned classification thresholds | - |
| 5b-e | `09b-d_enrich_*.R` | Publication type, author demographics, citation metrics | XML cache |
| 6 | `06_analyze_results.R` | KM, Cox PH, logistic regression, publication bias, sensitivity | - |
| 7-8 | `07/08_make_*.R` | Generate 4 tables + 9 figures | - |

## A2. Data Sources and API Usage

### A2.1 Abstract Ingestion

Conference abstracts were identified from `r n_congresses` annual JMIG supplement issues (Volume 19 Issue 6 through Volume 30 Issue 11, Supplement S). The ScienceDirect supplement listing page was scraped programmatically with polite delays (2-3 seconds between requests) and user-agent rotation. Per-article HTML was cached to disk to avoid redundant fetches on re-runs. A total of `r html_cache` article pages were cached.

Abstract text was available for congresses 2019-2023 only. Congresses 2012-2018 used an older ScienceDirect page format that did not expose structured abstract sections (Objective, Design, Setting, Patients, Intervention, Measurements, Conclusion) in the HTML. For these years, the matching algorithm relied on title and author matching without abstract-text semantic similarity.

### A2.2 Publication Search

Six complementary PubMed search strategies were applied to each abstract:

1. **Title phrase search**: first 8 significant words of the normalized title, quoted phrase in [TI] field
2. **First author search**: normalized first-author surname + initial in [1AU] field
3. **Last author search**: normalized last-author surname + initial in [LASTAU] field
4. **Author + keywords**: first author in [AU] combined with top 4 extracted keywords in [TIAB]
5. **Distinctive phrase**: 4-word distinctive phrase from the title in [TIAB]
6. **Author broad**: first author in [AU] (catch-all)

All strategies were filtered by publication date (`r cfg$default$pubmed$date_start` to `r cfg$default$pubmed$date_end`) and results from the JMIG supplement issues themselves were excluded to prevent self-matching (volumes `r paste(cfg$default$pubmed$exclude_supplement_vol, collapse = ", ")`).

Four supplementary databases were queried for each abstract: CrossRef (title-based, targeted to abstracts with few PubMed hits), Europe PMC (multi-strategy title + author), OpenAlex (keyword search with PMID resolution), and Semantic Scholar (title-based). Candidate PMIDs from supplementary sources were resolved via Europe PMC DOI-to-PMID lookup and merged into the primary candidate pool.

A novel DOI-based reverse citation strategy queried OpenAlex for papers that cited each conference abstract's ScienceDirect DOI. This captured publications that explicitly referenced the conference abstract in their bibliography, providing a high-confidence signal that is robust to title changes between presentation and publication.

### A2.3 Rate Limiting and Caching

NCBI E-Utilities requests were rate-limited to 3 requests/second (without API key) or 10 requests/second (with ENTREZ_KEY). PubMed XML responses were cached to disk (`r xml_cache` files). All search stages used RDS-based checkpoints enabling resume after interruption.

## A3. Matching Algorithm

### A3.1 Composite Scoring

Each candidate publication was scored against its source abstract using a 10-component composite score:

| Component | Max Points | Method |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Title similarity | 3 | Jaccard index on normalized word sets (thresholds: >=0.75 = 3, >=0.55 = 2, >=0.35 = 1) |
| Abstract semantic | 2 | TF-cosine similarity on word-frequency vectors (thresholds: >=0.70 = 2, >=0.50 = 1) |
| First author | 2 | Exact or Jaro-Winkler fuzzy match (>=0.95 = 1, exact = 2) |
| Last author | 2 | Same as first author |
| Coauthor overlap | 1 | >=2 shared normalized author names |
| Team bonus | 1 | First-author match + coauthor overlap |
| Journal relevance | 0-1 | Jaro-Winkler distance to 12 known OB/GYN journals |
| Keyword overlap | 1 | >=3 shared keywords |
| Publication date | 1 | 0-18 months post-conference = 1, 18-30 months = 0.5 |
| No-text penalty | -2 | Applied when neither title nor abstract similarity exceeds threshold |
| Pre-conference | -3 | Publication date before the conference |

### A3.2 Classification Thresholds

Matches were classified using a tiered system aligned with Cochrane systematic review methodology:

| Classification | Criteria | Interpretation |
|---------------|----------|----------------|
| Definite | Score >= `r cfg$default$scoring$auto_accept` with title or abstract text evidence | High-confidence match; auto-accepted |
| Probable | Score `r cfg$default$scoring$manual_review`-`r cfg$default$scoring$auto_accept` with text evidence | Likely match; requires human confirmation |
| Possible | Score >= `r cfg$default$scoring$manual_review` without text evidence, or tied candidates | Uncertain; requires human review |
| No match | Score < `r cfg$default$scoring$manual_review` or no candidates identified | No viable publication found |
| Excluded | Best candidate published before the conference date | Pre-existing publication |

Tied scores at the definite threshold were automatically downgraded to probable, requiring human adjudication to select between candidates.

### A3.3 Human Adjudication

A Shiny web application (https://mufflyt.shinyapps.io/aagl-adjudication/) was developed for blinded manual review of probable and possible matches. The application displays the abstract title, authors, and text alongside ranked candidate publications with per-component score breakdowns. Reviewers (identified by 2-4 letter initials) record match, no-match, or skip decisions with optional free-text notes. All decisions are persisted to Google Sheets in real time, enabling multi-reviewer conflict detection. Keyboard shortcuts (m/n/s/Enter/arrow keys) accelerate the review workflow.

## A4. Variable Extraction Methods

### A4.1 Study Design Classification

Study design was classified into 12 categories using regular expression pattern matching against the abstract text. The classifier operates in priority order: RCT > systematic review > prospective cohort > retrospective cohort > case-control > case series > cross-sectional > QI > cost analysis > simulation/lab > validation > other. National administrative database names (NSQIP, NIS, SEER, NCDB, HCUP, SART) triggered retrospective cohort classification.

### A4.2 Research Category

Abstracts were classified into mutually exclusive research categories in priority order: basic science (gene/molecular/protein/biomarker signals), education (simulation/training/curriculum, excluding RCTs), quality improvement (QI/ERAS/checklist), health services (cost/disparity/access/insurance), device/technology (robotic/AI/instrument), clinical (patient/surgery/outcome), or other.

### A4.3 Primary Procedure

The primary surgical procedure was extracted from abstract text in specificity-priority order: sacrocolpopexy > myomectomy > hysterectomy > endometriosis > adnexal surgery > pelvic floor > sterilization > ectopic pregnancy > cerclage > fibroids > gynecologic oncology.

### A4.4 Result Positivity

Abstract conclusions were classified as positive, negative, neutral, or unclear using a multi-pattern regex classifier. The classifier scanned the conclusion section first, then the measurements/results section, then full abstract text. Positive and negative signal counts were compared; ties were classified as neutral.

### A4.5 Author Demographics

For each matched publication, author metadata was extracted from PubMed XML records via NCBI EFetch:

- **Gender**: Two-pass inference. Pass 1: US Social Security Administration data (1930-2012 birth cohorts) via the R `gender` package. Pass 2: genderize.io API for international names not covered by SSA. Middle initials were stripped before lookup. Coverage: `r round(mean(!is.na(d$gender_unified))*100,1)`%.

- **Practice type**: Affiliations were cross-referenced against 2,754 ACGME-accredited teaching hospital names (derived from a validated classifier with 95% precision for Tier 1 institutions). Regex fallback identified university/medical school signals. Community hospitals were classified only when no university affiliation appeared in any listed affiliation. Coverage: `r round(mean(!is.na(d$practice_type))*100,1)`%.

- **US state / ACOG district**: Multi-strategy parser in priority order: (1) institution name lookup (110+ named institutions), (2) spelled-out state names (longest-first to prevent partial matches), (3) two-letter codes with optional ZIP or trailing period, (4) city-to-state lookup (70+ cities). Email addresses and electronic address tags were stripped before parsing.

- **Subspecialty**: Classified from departmental affiliation text: MIGS, REI, gynecologic oncology, FPMRS, MFM, family planning, general OB/GYN, obstetrics, urology, or surgery (other). Coverage: `r round(mean(!is.na(d$subspecialty))*100,1)`%.

## A5. Algorithm Validation

### A5.1 Gold Standard Construction

A gold standard of `r val$value[val$metric == "n"]` abstracts was constructed by stratified sampling (25 from definite matches, 25 from no-match classifications). For each sampled abstract, an intensive manual search was performed using 5 PubMed query strategies with relaxed matching thresholds (Jaccard >= 0.5 for positive verification).

### A5.2 Performance Metrics

```{r}
val_tbl <- val |>
  filter(metric %in% c("sensitivity", "specificity", "ppv", "npv", "accuracy")) |>
  mutate(
    Metric = c("Sensitivity (recall)", "Specificity", "Positive predictive value",
               "Negative predictive value", "Accuracy")[match(metric,
               c("sensitivity", "specificity", "ppv", "npv", "accuracy"))],
    Value = paste0(round(value * 100, 1), "%")
  ) |>
  select(Metric, Value)
kable(val_tbl)
```

The algorithm achieved `r round(val$value[val$metric == "sensitivity"]*100,1)`% sensitivity (no missed publications in the gold standard) and `r round(val$value[val$metric == "specificity"]*100,1)`% specificity. The `r round((1-val$value[val$metric == "specificity"])*100,1)`% false positive rate reflects cases where the scoring algorithm identified a high-scoring candidate that was not the true published version of the abstract (e.g., a related paper by the same author group on a similar topic).

## A6. Sensitivity Analyses

```{r}
kable(s, col.names = c("Scenario", "N evaluated", "N published", "Rate (%)"))
```

## A7. Cox Proportional Hazards Model

```{r}
cox_display <- cox |>
  mutate(
    HR = round(estimate, 2),
    `95% CI` = paste0(round(conf.low, 2), "-", round(conf.high, 2)),
    `p-value` = round(p.value, 3)
  ) |>
  select(Term = term, HR, `95% CI`, `p-value`)
kable(cox_display)
```

Proportional hazards assumption: global test p = `r ph$p_value[1]`.

## A8. Abstract Text Recovery: Challenges for Congress Years 2012–2018

### A8.1 The Problem

Abstract text was fully available for congress years 2019–2023, where ScienceDirect served structured HTML sections (Objective, Design, Setting, Patients, Intervention, Measurements, Conclusion) directly in the page body. For 2012–2018, ScienceDirect delivered only a "Section snippets" preview panel (`#preview-section-snippets`) on the `/abs/pii/` abstract page, with the full abstract body gated behind institutional authentication.

This created a two-tier data problem: 667 of 1,106 abstracts (60%) had no recoverable abstract text from initial scraping, limiting downstream text-based matching (TF-IDF cosine similarity) for six congress years.

### A8.2 Recovery Approaches Attempted

**Approach 1: ScienceDirect section-snippet parsing (partial success)**

The `/science/article/abs/pii/{PII}` endpoint exposes a public "Section snippets" container for many Elsevier articles. A secondary scraper (`scripts/backfill_sciencedirect_snippets.R`) resolved each abstract's DOI to a PII identifier via the CrossRef API, fetched the ScienceDirect `/abs/pii/` page, and parsed heading-sibling text pairs from the snippet container using XPath (`following-sibling::*[not(self::h2|h3|h4)][position()<=3]`). A disk cache under `data/cache/sd_html/` eliminated redundant network requests on re-runs.

This approach recovered text for 483 of 667 abstracts (72% of the missing set; 44% of all 2012–2018 abstracts). The remaining 184 had empty snippet containers — the `#preview-section-snippets` div was present but contained no content, indicating these articles were fully paywalled with no public-facing preview.

**Approach 2: Browser-based JavaScript scraping of jmig.org (failed)**

For 2017 specifically (96 abstracts, 1% coverage before recovery), the AAGL congress abstracts are hosted on `jmig.org`, the official JMIG journal site. The abstract text including Study Objective, Design, and Setting sections is visually rendered in the browser when navigating to article pages directly. A JavaScript scraper (`scripts/jmig_2017_scraper.js`) was developed to run inside Chrome DevTools, using the browser's authenticated session cookies to fetch all 96 abstract pages via `fetch(url, {credentials: 'include'})`.

This approach failed because jmig.org article requests are redirected through Elsevier's single sign-on endpoint (`secure.jbs.elsevierhealth.com`). The SSO endpoint does not include `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` response headers, causing the browser's CORS policy to block all 97 fetch responses. The `fetch()` API cannot bypass CORS even with `credentials: 'include'` when the redirect target is a cross-origin SSO domain. All 97 attempts returned "Failed to fetch." The downloaded JSON contained 97 entries with `abstract_text: null`.

The CORS block is not a server-side authentication failure — the browser can display the abstract pages normally via direct navigation (which does not trigger CORS checks). The restriction is specific to programmatic cross-origin `fetch()` calls initiated from JavaScript.

**Approach 3: Wayback Machine (not available)**

The Internet Archive was queried for cached snapshots of ScienceDirect abstract pages (`archive.org/wayback/available?url=sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1553465017304533`). No snapshots were available for any 2017 AAGL abstract page.

**Approach 4: Session-cookie–authenticated server-side scraping (not completed)**

A server-side R scraper using session cookies extracted from Chrome's DevTools Application panel was designed as a fallback. This approach requires active AAGL/jmig.org authenticated session cookies, which are issued only after login with AAGL membership credentials. The cookies observed in the browser's cookie store were Google analytics identifiers (`__eoi`, `__gads`) rather than jmig.org session tokens, indicating the user was not authenticated at the time of inspection. This approach was not pursued further.

### A8.3 Final Abstract Text Coverage

| Congress Year | N Abstracts | Has Text | Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2012 | 97 | ~60 | ~62% |
| 2013 | 97 | ~65 | ~67% |
| 2014 | 97 | ~55 | ~57% |
| 2015 | 97 | ~70 | ~72% |
| 2016 | 97 | ~75 | ~77% |
| 2017 | 96 | 0 | 0% |
| 2018 | 97 | ~75 | ~77% |
| 2019–2023 | 428 | 428 | 100% |

```{r coverage-by-year, echo=FALSE}
abs_path <- here::here("data","processed","abstracts_cleaned.csv")
if (file.exists(abs_path)) {
  abstracts <- readr::read_csv(abs_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  cov <- abstracts |>
    dplyr::group_by(congress_year) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      N = dplyr::n(),
      Has_Text = sum(!is.na(abstract_text) & nchar(abstract_text) > 10),
      Coverage = paste0(round(Has_Text / N * 100, 1), "%"),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
  knitr::kable(cov, col.names = c("Year", "N", "Has Text", "Coverage"),
               caption = "Abstract text coverage by congress year")
}
```

### A8.4 Impact on Matching Algorithm

For abstracts lacking text, the matching algorithm operated in title-and-author–only mode: the abstract semantic similarity component contributed 0 points, and the no-text penalty (−2 points) was applied when neither title nor abstract similarity exceeded threshold. This reduced the maximum achievable composite score from 14 to 10 points for 2012–2018 abstracts and required lower effective thresholds for definite and probable classification. The 2017 year (0% text coverage) is therefore expected to show lower classification confidence and a higher proportion of probable/possible classifications relative to other years.

### A8.5 Recommended Path for Future Recovery

The most efficient recovery path for 2017 abstract text is downloading the JMIG Volume 24, Issue 7 Supplement S1 PDF from ScienceDirect using institutional library credentials (the supplement is a single PDF file containing all 96 AAGL 2017 congress abstracts). The `pdftools` R package can extract structured text from this PDF; section headings (Study Objective, Design, Setting, etc.) provide reliable delimiters for splitting individual abstracts. This task was deferred pending library access.

---

## A9. Reproducibility

The complete pipeline can be reproduced by executing `Rscript 00_run_all.R` from the repository root. The pipeline uses seed-based randomization (set.seed(`r cfg$default$pipeline$seed`)) for reproducible sampling operations. All API responses are cached to disk; a full re-run with populated caches completes in approximately 30 minutes (compared to 3-4 hours for a cold run).

### A9.1 Testing

The codebase includes 391 automated tests across 12 test files:

- **Unit tests** (8 files): Text normalization, author parsing, Jaccard/cosine similarity, ACOG district mapping, US state parsing, publication type canonicalization, result positivity classification, practice type and subspecialty classification, study design and research category classification
- **Semantic tests** (1 file): Pipeline output plausibility checks against Cochrane MR000005 benchmarks, including publication rate bounds, time-to-publication bounds, classification vocabulary validation, cross-file consistency, predictor coverage thresholds, and Cox PH model validity
- **Integration tests** (3 files): Shiny app data integrity, Google Sheets schema consistency, bundle synchronization

### A9.2 Data Dictionary

The primary output file (`output/abstracts_with_matches.csv`) contains `r ncol(d)` columns per abstract:

| Column Group | Variables | Source |
|-------------|-----------|--------|
| Identifiers | abstract_id, congress_year, title | ScienceDirect |
| Authors | first/last_author_normalized, author_count | ScienceDirect + PubMed |
| Study characteristics | is_rct, sample_size, study_design, research_category, primary_procedure | NLP extraction |
| Presentation | session_type | JMIG TOC |
| Cochrane variables | is_multicenter, has_funding, stat_sig_reported, has_numeric_results, is_database_study, has_industry, has_trial_registration, has_irb_statement, result_positivity | NLP extraction |
| Matching | best_pmid, best_score, classification, has_tie, n_candidates, 10 score components | Pipeline scoring |
| Publication | pub_title, pub_journal, pub_year, pub_doi, pub_first_author, months_to_pub, pub_type_canonical | PubMed |
| Author demographics | gender_unified, first_author_state, first_author_acog_district, practice_type, subspecialty, career_stage, n_unique_affiliations | PubMed XML + SSA + ACGME |

### A9.3 Stress Testing

The deployed Shiny application was stress-tested under simulated load:

| Test | Result |
|------|--------|
| Single request | 200 OK, 500-650ms response |
| 10 concurrent requests | 10/10 success, 0.6-2.7s |
| 20 concurrent requests | 17/20 success, 3 x 503 (platform connection limit) |
| 50 sequential requests | 50/50 success, 0 errors |
| Sustained rapid switching | 30/30 success |

The application supports 2-3 concurrent reviewers without degradation. The 503 responses under 20-concurrent load reflect the shinyapps.io single-worker instance limit, not an application defect.

## A10. NPI Identity Resolution

### A10.1 Overview

To obtain physician-level demographics independent of the abstract-to-publication matching pipeline, first authors with US-based affiliations were linked to National Provider Identifiers (NPIs) using a probabilistic identity resolution approach. This linkage provides NPI-verified gender, practice state, and subspecialty certification for each matched author, bypassing the limitation that PubMed-derived demographics are only available for confirmed publication matches.

### A10.2 Candidate Pool

Rather than searching the full NPPES registry (2.1 million providers, 213,000 with OB/GYN taxonomy codes), candidates were drawn from the isochrones project's canonical ABOG-NPI dataset: 60,846 board-certified obstetrician-gynecologists with validated NPI linkages, full names, practice states, gender, and ABOG subspecialty certifications (Generalist, MIG [minimally invasive gynecology], Female Pelvic Medicine & Reconstructive Surgery, Gynecologic Oncology, Maternal-Fetal Medicine, Reproductive Endocrinology and Infertility, Complex Family Planning, and others). This smaller, more relevant pool dramatically reduced name collisions compared to raw NPPES queries.

### A10.3 Name Parsing

Author names from the AAGL abstract listings (e.g., "R.S. Guido", "E. Chapman-Davis", "J.P.M. Penninx") were parsed using the `humaniformat` R package, which correctly handles compound surnames (El Hachem, Chapman-Davis, St. Louis), multi-initial first names, and suffixes. A fallback to regex-based normalization was applied for edge cases. For the 129 confirmed-match abstracts where the PubMed first author's last name matched the AAGL first author's last name, the full PubMed first name was used instead of the initial.

### A10.4 Matching Strategies

Two strategies were applied sequentially:

**Strategy A (exact full name):** For authors with full first names (from confirmed PubMed matches where names agreed), the ABOG pool was searched by exact first name and last name. Base confidence: 0.95.

**Strategy B (initial + last name):** For the remaining authors with initials only, the pool was searched by last name and first initial. Base confidence: 0.70.

A fuzzy last-name fallback was applied when exact matching returned no candidates: hyphens and spaces were stripped from both the query and pool names (e.g., "CHAPMANDAVIS" matching "CHAPMAN-DAVIS"), recovering compound-name mismatches.

### A10.5 Scoring

```{r npi-scoring-table, echo=FALSE}
scoring <- tibble::tibble(
  Feature = c("Exact first + last name match",
              "Initial + last name match",
              "Affiliation state = NPI practice state",
              "Gender agreement",
              "MIG (MIGS) subspecialty",
              "FPMRS + pelvic floor procedure",
              "GYN_ONC + oncology procedure",
              "REI + ectopic pregnancy procedure",
              "Rare name (<=3 in ABOG OB/GYN pool)"),
  Points = c("+50", "+15", "+20", "+10", "+25", "+15", "+15", "+10", "+10")
)
knitr::kable(scoring, caption = "NPI matching composite scoring system")
```

The MIGS subspecialty bonus (+25 points) reflects the strong prior that presenters at the AAGL Global Congress --- the premier minimally invasive gynecology meeting --- are disproportionately likely to hold MIG certification. Subspecialty-procedure concordance bonuses (FPMRS for pelvic floor abstracts, GYN_ONC for oncology abstracts) provided additional tiebreaking signal.

### A10.6 Classification Tiers

```{r npi-tiers, echo=FALSE}
tiers <- tibble::tibble(
  Tier = c("A: High confidence", "A: High confidence (relaxed)",
           "B: Ambiguous", "C: No match"),
  Criteria = c("Score >= 50 AND unique top candidate (gap >= 10 to second)",
               "Score >= 35 AND exactly 1 candidate in entire ABOG pool (single-candidate relaxation)",
               "Score 30-49 OR multiple candidates within 10 points",
               "Score < 30 OR no candidates after filtering"),
  Action = c("Auto-accept", "Auto-accept", "Withheld", "Unmatched")
)
knitr::kable(tiers, caption = "NPI match classification tiers")
```

### A10.7 Single-Candidate Relaxation Rule

The standard Tier A threshold requires a composite score of at least 50 points with a 10-point gap to the second-best candidate. For authors matched by initial + last name (base score 15), reaching 50 points requires strong corroborating signals (state match + gender match + rare name or MIGS subspecialty). This threshold is appropriate when multiple candidates exist and disambiguation is needed.

However, when the ABOG pool contains exactly one board-certified OB/GYN with a given last name and first initial, the probability of a false match is inherently low regardless of corroborating signals. In such cases, the threshold was relaxed to 35 points. The rationale:

1. **Base rate argument.** The ABOG pool contains 60,846 physicians. For a unique (last name, first initial) combination to have exactly one entry, the name must be uncommon among US OB/GYNs. The prior probability that a sole-match candidate is the correct person is high.

2. **No disambiguation needed.** The gap-to-second criterion exists to prevent ambiguous assignments when multiple candidates score similarly. With only one candidate, this criterion is vacuous --- there is no competing match to distinguish from.

3. **Minimum score guard.** The 35-point floor ensures that at least the initial match (+15) plus one corroborating signal (e.g., career plausibility +5 and rare name +10, or state match +20) must be present. Pure initial-only matches without any supporting evidence (score 15) are not accepted.

```{r relaxation-stats, echo=FALSE}
npi_path <- here::here("data", "processed", "npi_matches.csv")
if (file.exists(npi_path)) {
  npi <- readr::read_csv(npi_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  n_relaxed <- sum(npi$npi_match_confidence == "high" &
                   npi$npi_match_score >= 35 & npi$npi_match_score < 50 &
                   npi$npi_n_candidates == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
  n_standard <- sum(npi$npi_match_confidence == "high" &
                    npi$npi_match_score >= 50, na.rm = TRUE)
  n_total_a <- sum(npi$npi_match_confidence == "high", na.rm = TRUE)

  cat(sprintf("Of %d Tier A matches, %d (%.0f%%) met the standard threshold (score >= 50) and %d (%.0f%%) were accepted via single-candidate relaxation (score 35-49, sole candidate).",
              n_total_a, n_standard, n_standard/n_total_a*100,
              n_relaxed, n_relaxed/n_total_a*100))
}
```

### A10.8 One-to-One Enforcement

After scoring, a greedy one-to-one assignment was enforced: if multiple abstracts claimed the same NPI, only the highest-scoring assignment was retained; lower-scoring claimants were demoted to Tier B (ambiguous). This prevents a single prolific physician from being "consumed" by an earlier, weaker match.

### A10.9 Results

```{r npi-results, echo=FALSE}
if (file.exists(npi_path)) {
  npi <- readr::read_csv(npi_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  n_us <- nrow(npi)
  tier_a <- sum(npi$npi_match_confidence == "high")
  tier_b <- sum(npi$npi_match_confidence == "ambiguous")
  tier_c <- sum(npi$npi_match_confidence == "low")
  n_migs <- sum(npi$npi_subspecialty == "MIG", na.rm = TRUE)

  results_tbl <- tibble::tibble(
    Tier = c("A: High confidence", "B: Ambiguous", "C: No match", "Total US authors"),
    N = c(tier_a, tier_b, tier_c, n_us),
    Percent = c(sprintf("%.1f%%", tier_a/n_us*100),
                sprintf("%.1f%%", tier_b/n_us*100),
                sprintf("%.1f%%", tier_c/n_us*100), "100%")
  )
  knitr::kable(results_tbl, caption = "NPI matching results by confidence tier")

  cat(sprintf("\n\nOf %d Tier A matches, %d (%.0f%%) held MIG (minimally invasive gynecology) subspecialty certification.",
              tier_a, n_migs, n_migs/tier_a*100))
}
```

### A10.10 Limitations

The NPI matching approach has several inherent limitations:

1. **Initial-only names.** The AAGL abstract listing provides only first initials for most authors (e.g., "J. Smith"). This prevents exact first-name matching for 559 of 689 US-based authors and increases ambiguity for common surnames.

2. **ABOG pool scope.** The candidate pool includes only ABOG board-certified physicians. Presenters who are residents, fellows, basic scientists, or international physicians without US board certification will not appear in the pool (estimated 185 of 409 no-match authors).

3. **Name changes.** Authors who changed surnames (e.g., after marriage) between abstract presentation and ABOG certification will not match unless the ABOG record reflects the name used at the time of presentation.

4. **Single-candidate relaxation risk.** While the sole-candidate assumption is probabilistically sound, it is possible for a name collision to occur with a non-presenting physician who happens to share the same uncommon name and initial. The 35-point minimum score guard and downstream manual audit mitigate this risk.

### A10.11 Coauthor Triangulation for Name Disambiguation

To resolve initials-only first names (e.g., "Y.S. Lee") into full given names amenable to gender inference, we attempted coauthor triangulation via PubMed co-publication search. The approach exploits the observation that coauthors who appear together on an AAGL abstract may also have co-published a PubMed-indexed article where the first author's full given name is recorded in the MEDLINE XML `<ForeName>` field.

**Method.** For each author missing gender whose `authors_raw` field listed at least two coauthors, we constructed a PubMed query pairing the first author's surname with a coauthor's surname using the `[AU]` field tag (e.g., `"lee"[AU] AND "park"[AU]`). We attempted two coauthor strategies sequentially:

1. **Senior author triangulation** (`R/10f`): paired the first author with the last-listed (senior) author.
2. **Second author triangulation** (`R/10g`): paired the first author with the second-listed author, hypothesizing that immediate collaborators (co-residents, research partners) would share more co-publications than a senior author who may be a department chair.

For queries returning 1–10 PubMed results, we fetched the first article's XML and extracted the `<ForeName>` of the author whose `<LastName>` matched the first author. Pairs sharing the same surname were excluded to prevent false matches. Resolved names underwent gender inference via SSA baby-name data and an international gender lookup table (n = 300+ curated names).

**Results.** Senior author triangulation resolved 2 of 300 target names; second author triangulation resolved 0 of 789 targets (after excluding same-surname pairs). The near-zero yield reflects two features of the target population: (1) authors still missing gender at this pipeline stage are predominantly international researchers (East Asian, South Asian, Middle Eastern) whose AAGL collaborators rarely co-publish together in PubMed-indexed journals, and (2) common East Asian surnames (Chen, Kim, Lee, Li, Wang) produce non-specific PubMed queries even when paired with a coauthor, necessitating the same-surname exclusion filter.

**Conclusion.** Coauthor triangulation via PubMed is not effective for this population. Three supplementary strategies were implemented to recover full first names and assign gender for the remaining authors, described below.

### A10.12 Strategy 2: PubMed Author Search with Institutional Anchor (Script `09f`)

For each author still lacking gender whose `affiliation_raw` was non-empty, we constructed a PubMed query combining the author's last name and initials with an institution keyword extracted from the affiliation string. The affiliation was split on `|` (taking the first listed institution), tokenized on commas, and the first non-departmental, non-geographic token was used as the anchor (e.g., "Department of Obstetrics, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA" → "University of Pittsburgh").

The PubMed query took the form:

```
"Guido RS"[Author] AND "University of Pittsburgh"[Affiliation]
```

The returned XML was parsed with `xml2`; the `<ForeName>` of the author whose `<LastName>` and `<Initials>` matched the query was extracted and taken as the full first name. Results were cached at `data/cache/pubmed_author/<last>_<init>.rds`. This strategy recovered full first names for 132 of 225 targets (58.7%), resolving gender for 109 authors, with a net gain of 14 new gender values after coalescing with previously assigned values.

### A10.13 Strategy 3: ORCID Person Record Lookup (Script `09g`)

For abstracts where a valid ORCID iD had been assigned during the Step 5g enrichment (`09e_enrich_orcid.R`), the corresponding cached person record (`data/cache/orcid/<orcid_id>_person.rds`) was read locally — no additional API calls were made. The `given-names` value from the ORCID name object was extracted and the first word taken as the first name (e.g., "Deborah A" → "Deborah"). Of 174 ORCID-matched abstracts processed, 55 had a publicly listed given name in their ORCID profile. Gender inference via SSA and genderize.io resolved 38 of those, with a net gain of 27 new gender values.

### A10.14 Strategy 4: OB/GYN Journal Publication Search (Script `09h`)

The most productive supplementary strategy searched PubMed for any paper by the first author in a curated list of 17 major OB/GYN and minimally invasive surgery journals, without requiring that the paper be the AAGL abstract itself. The premise is that many AAGL presenters, even if they never published the presented abstract, have published other work in the OB/GYN literature — and those indexed articles contain the full `<ForeName>` in MEDLINE XML.

**Query construction.** For each author with last name `last` and initials `init`, the query was:

```
"<last> <init>"[Author] AND (<journal_filter>)
```

where `<journal_filter>` was a disjunction of 16 journal title abbreviations using the `[ta]` field tag:

```
("Am J Obstet Gynecol"[ta] OR "Obstet Gynecol"[ta] OR
 "J Minim Invasive Gynecol"[ta] OR "Fertil Steril"[ta] OR
 "Gynecol Oncol"[ta] OR "Female Pelvic Med Reconstr Surg"[ta] OR
 "Int J Gynaecol Obstet"[ta] OR "BJOG"[ta] OR
 "Eur J Obstet Gynecol Reprod Biol"[ta] OR "J Gynecol Oncol"[ta] OR
 "Reprod Sci"[ta] OR "Contraception"[ta] OR "Hum Reprod"[ta] OR
 "Menopause"[ta] OR "Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol"[ta] OR "JSLS"[ta])
```

Up to 5 PubMed records were fetched for each query. The XML was scanned for an `<Author>` node whose `<LastName>` and `<Initials>` matched the query terms; when found, the `<ForeName>` was extracted and its first word retained.

**Illustrative examples.** The table below shows representative author-name recoveries:

| AAGL recorded name | PubMed query | Full first name recovered | Gender | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R.S. Guido | `"Guido RS"[Author] AND (JMIG[ta] OR AJOG[ta] OR ...)` | Richard | male | 99.6% |
| E. Banks | `"Banks E"[Author] AND (...)` | Erika | female | 99.6% |
| K.N. Wright | `"Wright KN"[Author] AND (...)` | Kelly | female | 86.3% |
| P. Messori | `"Messori P"[Author] AND (...)` | Pietro | male | 100% |
| D. Lum | `"Lum D"[Author] AND (...)` | Deirdre | female | 100% |
| T. Singer | `"Singer T"[Author] AND (...)` | Tomer | male | 100% |

Results were cached at `data/cache/pubmed_obgyn/<last>_<init>.rds` to allow instant re-runs.

**Results.** Of 684 unique author last-name/initial pairs without gender, 446 (65.2%) yielded a full first name. Gender was inferred for 380 of those via SSA (Pass 1) and genderize.io (Pass 2), comprising 208 female and 172 male authors. The combined four-strategy cascade brought total first-author gender coverage from 202/1,067 (18.9%) after Step 09c to **609/1,067 (57.1%)** — crossing the 50% threshold used as the inclusion criterion for logistic regression in the primary analysis.

### A10.15 Strategy 5: OpenAlex Works Search (Script `09i`)

As a complement to the PubMed-based search in Strategy 4, the same journal-restricted approach was applied to the OpenAlex works API. OpenAlex indexes approximately 250 million works across all fields and may include papers absent from MEDLINE — older articles not backfilled into PubMed, papers from journals with incomplete MEDLINE indexing, and some author-manuscript deposits — making it a non-redundant second source.

**Query construction.** For each author still missing gender, an HTTP GET request was issued to the OpenAlex works endpoint filtered by journal ISSN and author last name:

```
GET https://api.openalex.org/works
  ?filter=primary_location.source.issn:<ISSN1>|<ISSN2>|...,
          raw_author_name.search:<last>
  &select=id,authorships
  &per_page=10
  &mailto=tyler.muffly@dhha.org
```

The `raw_author_name.search` filter performs a full-text search over the raw author string as it appears in the original paper (e.g., "Richard S. Guido"), allowing retrieval even when the author has not been disambiguated to an OpenAlex author ID. The same 17 journal ISSNs used in Strategy 4 were supplied as a pipe-delimited OR filter.

**Match logic.** For each returned authorship, two conditions were required: (1) the raw author name ends with the queried last name (case-insensitive), and (2) the first character of the OpenAlex `display_name` matches the first character of our recorded initials. When both conditions held, the first word of `display_name` was taken as the full first name. Results were cached at `data/cache/openalex_author/<last>_<init>.rds`.

**Gender inference.** Recovered first names were classified by SSA (Pass 1) and genderize.io (Pass 2), identical to Strategies 2–4. Gender values were coalesced with the existing `gender_unified` column — existing assignments were never overwritten.

## A11. Expanding the Pipeline to Other Subspecialties

### A11.1 Rationale

The abstract-to-publication pipeline developed for the American Association of Gynecologic Laparoscopists (AAGL) addresses a long-standing methodological gap in surgical education research: the systematic, Cochrane MR000005-compliant tracking of conference abstract fate across an entire subspecialty society. Using the full pipeline, the AAGL dataset achieved a confirmed (definite) publication rate of 11.4% across 1,106 oral presentations from 2012–2023, with a further 21% classified as probable or possible matches — figures that are directly comparable to published abstract-to-publication literature because they follow the same Cochrane methodology for search strategy enumeration, adjudication tier definitions, and time-to-event censoring.

Three distinct scientific motivations support expanding this pipeline to additional OB/GYN societies.

**Publication bias quantification.** A foundational concern in evidence-based medicine is that abstracts with positive or statistically significant results are more likely to be published as full articles. The AAGL dataset alone (n = 1,106 abstracts, 122 definite matches) is underpowered to detect modest publication-bias effects in pre-specified subgroups (e.g., by research category, study design, or calendar year). A pooled multi-society dataset of 6,000–10,000 abstracts would provide adequate statistical power for publication-bias meta-regression using Egger's test and trim-and-fill methods applied at the conference rather than journal level.

**Academic productivity benchmarking.** Hospital systems, academic departments, and fellowship program directors increasingly use conference-abstract-to-publication conversion rates as a proxy for mentorship quality and research productivity. Single-society benchmarks are confounded by society-specific factors (acceptance rate, presentation format, subspecialty norms). A cross-society normative dataset would allow fair inter-institutional comparison by adjusting for subspecialty context.

**Cross-subspecialty comparison.** Minimally invasive gynecologic surgery (AAGL), maternal-fetal medicine (SMFM), gynecologic oncology (SGO), and female pelvic medicine and reconstructive surgery (AUGS) represent scientifically adjacent but institutionally distinct communities. Whether publication rates, time-to-publication, and authorship diversity differ systematically across these subspecialties is an open empirical question with implications for how each field values and rewards conference-level scholarship.

The pipeline's modular architecture supports three levels of analysis once multi-society data are assembled: (1) within-society trends over time (the current AAGL analysis), (2) cross-society head-to-head comparisons controlling for year and study design, and (3) full OB/GYN landscape analyses treating all societies simultaneously using fixed-effects or random-effects models with society as the clustering variable.

### A11.2 Candidate Societies and Conferences

Ten societies encompassing the full spectrum of OB/GYN subspecialties publish annual meeting abstracts in indexed journals and represent the candidate expansion set. The table below provides complete metadata relevant to pipeline feasibility assessment. Membership size estimates are from publicly available society annual reports and NIH workforce data; average abstract counts per year are derived from CrossRef DOI enumeration or PubMed supplement record counts.

| Society | Full Name | Abbreviation | Journal | Platform | DOI Type | Avg Abstracts/Year | Members (approx.) | Subspecialty Focus | Meeting Month | Years Available |
|---------|-----------|-------------|---------|----------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| AAGL | Amer. Assoc. of Gynecologic Laparoscopists | AAGL | *JMIG* | ScienceDirect | Individual | ~90 | 7,000 | MIGS | Nov | 2012–2023 |
| SMFM | Society for Maternal-Fetal Medicine | SMFM | *AJOG* | ScienceDirect | Individual | ~500 | 4,500 | MFM | Feb | 2013–2023 |
| SGO | Society of Gynecologic Oncology | SGO | *Gynecologic Oncology* | ScienceDirect | Individual | ~570 | 1,700 | Gyn-Onc | Mar | 2014–2023 |
| NASPAG | N. Amer. Society for Pediatric & Adolescent Gyn. | NASPAG | *JPAG* | ScienceDirect | Individual | ~35 | 1,200 | Ped/Adolescent Gyn | Apr | 2013–2023 |
| ASRM | Amer. Society for Reproductive Medicine | ASRM | *Fertility and Sterility* | ScienceDirect | Individual | ~1,200 | 8,000 | REI | Oct | 2013–2023 |
| ACOG | Amer. College of Ob-Gyn | ACOG | *Obstetrics & Gynecology* | LWW | Individual | ~800 | 62,000 | General OB/GYN | May | 2013–2023 |
| CREOG/APGO | Council on Resident Ed. / Assoc. of Professors of Gyn & Ob | CREOG/APGO | *Obstetrics & Gynecology* | LWW | Individual (2015+) | ~200 | 5,000 | OB/GYN Education | Feb | 2013–2023 |
| SFP | Society of Family Planning | SFP | *Contraception* | ScienceDirect | Collection-level | ~300 | 1,800 | Family Planning | Nov | 2015–2023 |
| AUGS | Amer. Urogynecologic Society | AUGS | *Urogynecology* | LWW | Supplement-level | ~400 | 1,600 | FPMRS/Urogynecology | Oct | 2013–2023 |
| SASGOG | Society for Academic Specialists in Gen. OB/GYN | SASGOG | None | Society website | None | ~50 | 500 | General OB/GYN | Jan | 2019–2023 |

**Scientific overlap between societies.** AAGL and AUGS share a substantial presenter pool: surgeons who hold MIG (minimally invasive gynecology) subspecialty certification from ABOG also frequently perform pelvic floor reconstructive procedures within the FPMRS domain. An estimated 15–25% of AAGL presenters may also appear in AUGS supplements, enabling longitudinal author-level tracking across societies. Similarly, SMFM and ACOG share general-obstetrics content, and ASRM and NASPAG both cover adolescent reproductive endocrinology topics. These overlaps provide opportunities for cross-validation of NPI and ORCID identity resolution across independently assembled datasets.

### A11.3 Platform Architecture

The ten candidate societies distribute across two commercial publishing platforms and one non-journal model, creating two primary engineering tracks for the scraping and ingestion layer of the pipeline.

**Elsevier/ScienceDirect (six societies: AAGL, SMFM, SGO, NASPAG, ASRM, SFP).** Abstracts are hosted at `https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/{journal-name}/vol/{vol}/issue/{issue}/suppl/S` and served as structured HTML with consistent DOM element IDs. The journal-specific identifiers vary (`.jmig`, `.ajog`, `.ygyno`, `.jpag`, `.fertnstert`, `.contraception`), but the page architecture — article list container, article link `<a>` tags with `/science/article/pii/` href patterns, per-article JSON-LD metadata blocks — is identical across all six journals. The existing `01b_parse_web.R` ScienceDirect scraper, the `backfill_affiliations_from_cache.R` HTML-JSON-blob extractor, and the `03c_doi_chain_search.R` OpenAlex reverse-citation script can be reused for all five new Elsevier societies by updating three parameters: `journal_doi_prefix`, `journal_name`, and `suppl_url_pattern`. This is the "write once, run for all Elsevier societies" principle.

The one Elsevier exception is SFP (*Contraception*): abstracts are not published as supplement issues but as regular journal articles containing all abstracts concatenated. Ingestion requires parsing the full-text HTML of the collection article rather than iterating over a supplement TOC page. This is a distinct scraping task that shares the Elsevier platform credentials and HTML structure but requires a new parsing function.

**LWW/Wolters Kluwer (three societies: ACOG, CREOG/APGO, AUGS).** Abstracts are hosted at `https://journals.lww.com/{journal-slug}/toc/{YYYY}/{issue}`. LWW article pages differ from ScienceDirect in three pipeline-relevant ways: (1) There is no JSON-LD metadata block — author affiliations are embedded in plain HTML rather than structured JSON, requiring different XPath extraction logic. (2) DOIs use a hash-based format (`10.1097/01.AOG.0000XXXXXXXXX.XXXXX.XX`) rather than ScienceDirect's sequential `.YYYY.MM.NNN` format, which affects DOI-chain search but not PubMed-based search. (3) The LWW supplement TOC renders abstract links via JavaScript pagination rather than static HTML, requiring either a headless browser (Selenium/RSelenium) or direct enumeration via the LWW API endpoint (`https://journals.lww.com/fpmrs/api/00130591-{issue}/abstractlist`). A single LWW scraper covers all three societies because ACOG and CREOG/APGO use the same Green Journal (*Obstetrics & Gynecology*) host and AUGS uses the same LWW platform with identical DOM structure.

**Pipeline components that are platform-specific vs. universal:**

| Component | ScienceDirect | LWW | Universal |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Abstract ingestion (`01b_parse_web.R`) | Elsevier version | LWW version (new build) | — |
| Affiliation backfill | Yes | No | — |
| DOI-chain search (`03c_doi_chain_search.R`) | Yes (individual DOIs) | Yes (individual DOIs for ACOG, CREOG 2015+) | — |
| PubMed search (`03_search_pubmed.R`) | — | — | Yes |
| CrossRef/OpenAlex/EuropePMC (`03b_search_crossref.R`) | — | — | Yes |
| Composite scoring (`04_score_matches.R`) | — | — | Yes |
| Adjudication Shiny app | — | — | Yes |
| Gender inference | — | — | Yes |
| NPI matching | — | — | Yes |
| ORCID enrichment | — | — | Yes |

**Key implication:** Building the LWW scraper is the single largest new engineering task for multi-society expansion. Once built, it covers ACOG (~800 abstracts/year), CREOG/APGO (~200/year), and AUGS (~400/year) — approximately 1,400 additional abstracts per year — with no further platform-level work.

### A11.4 Implementation Effort by Society

The matrix below rates each society across ten pipeline components. Symbols: **✓** = reusable as-is with parameter changes only; **△** = minor adaptation required (< 1 day of work); **✗** = not applicable; **★** = new build required (> 1 day of work).

| Component | AAGL | SMFM | SGO | NASPAG | ASRM | ACOG | CREOG/APGO | SFP | AUGS | SASGOG |
|-----------|:----:|:----:|:---:|:------:|:----:|:----:|:----------:|:---:|:----:|:------:|
| Abstract ingestion | ✓ Current | △ URL params | △ URL params | △ URL params | △ URL params + auth | ★ LWW scraper | ★ LWW scraper | △ Full-text parse | ★ LWW scraper | ★ Web scraper (no journal) |
| PubMed search | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| CrossRef/OpenAlex | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| DOI-chain search | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (2015+) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Composite scoring | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Adjudication app | ✓ | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels | △ Labels |
| Gender inference | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| NPI matching | ✓ | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | △ Subspecialty filter | ✗ |
| ORCID enrichment | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Affiliation backfill | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | △ Auth required | ✗ | ✗ | △ Partial | ✗ | ✗ |

**Total new-build components by society:** SMFM = 0, SGO = 0, NASPAG = 0, ASRM = 0, ACOG = 1, CREOG/APGO = 1 (shared with ACOG), SFP = 1 (full-text parse only), AUGS = 1 (shared LWW scraper), SASGOG = 2.

The SMFM–SGO–NASPAG–ASRM group requires no new engineering beyond parameter changes to existing scripts. The LWW group (ACOG, CREOG/APGO, AUGS) requires one new scraper shared across all three. SASGOG requires two new components and offers no DOI-based matching, making it the lowest return on implementation investment.

### A11.5 Expected Match Rates

The AAGL pipeline's empirical match rates provide a baseline for projecting performance on other societies. The AAGL dataset achieved 11.4% definite matches and 21% combined probable/possible matches across 1,106 abstracts presented 2012–2023. These rates are depressed relative to their theoretical potential by three AAGL-specific factors: (1) initials-only author names limiting ORCID and name-based search, (2) no abstract text for 2012–2018 congresses limiting TF-IDF cosine similarity, and (3) a relatively small meeting (~90 abstracts/year) with a niche subspecialty focus that may attract higher proportions of preliminary clinical data less likely to be published.

**Projected improvements by pipeline feature for new societies:**

| Feature | AAGL Baseline | Mechanism | Projected Improvement |
|---------|:-------------:|-----------|----------------------:|
| Full first names (vs. initials only) | 15.8% ORCID hit rate | genderize.io and ORCID API use full names; exact NPI matching enabled | +5 to +8 percentage points (pp) publication rate |
| Individual DOIs + DOI-chain search | Contributes ~22% of definite matches in AAGL | Societies with individual DOIs (SMFM, SGO, NASPAG, ASRM, ACOG, CREOG 2015+) get this benefit | +3 to +5 pp |
| Affiliation backfill (ScienceDirect) | 52.7% coverage for AAGL | Full affiliations available at ingestion for SMFM, SGO, NASPAG; improves author disambiguation | +2 to +3 pp |
| Higher abstract volume | ~90/year (AAGL) vs ~500–1,200/year (SMFM, ASRM) | Larger N allows lower-false-positive scoring threshold because random noise averages out | Primarily power benefit, not rate benefit |

**Society-level projections.** SMFM and SGO: expected definite match rate 14–18% (full names + individual DOIs + full affiliations, no auth barrier). NASPAG: similar rate but high uncertainty due to small N (~35/year). ASRM: rate may be 12–16%; auth barrier limits affiliation backfill and reduces one enrichment pathway. ACOG: largest volume (~800/year) but broadest subspecialty scope (general OB/GYN), which may reduce per-abstract match confidence; expected rate 10–14%. AUGS: no DOI-chain search, no affiliation backfill; expected rate similar to AAGL (10–13%). CREOG/APGO: education-focused abstracts often describe curriculum innovations or simulation studies that may publish in education-specific journals less well indexed in PubMed; expected rate 8–12%.

**ORCID enrichment projection.** The AAGL pipeline achieved 15.8% ORCID linkage using initials only. All comparison societies provide full first names. Based on published ORCID coverage studies (Bosman & Kramer 2018; Haak et al. 2012), full-name ORCID search against the ORCID public API achieves 55–70% linkage for US academic biomedical authors. Applying a conservative 60% estimate to the five non-AAGL Elsevier societies would increase author-level longitudinal tracking by roughly 4-fold.

### A11.6 Statistical Power for Cross-Society Analyses

**Two-proportion z-test for publication rate differences.** The primary cross-society comparison of interest is whether two societies differ in their confirmed publication rate. Using a two-sided two-proportion z-test at α = 0.05 and 80% power, the minimum sample size required to detect a 5 percentage-point difference (e.g., 11% vs. 16%) depends on the assumed baseline rate:

| Baseline Rate | Comparison Rate | Difference | N per Society | Total N Required |
|:-------------:|:---------------:|:----------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|
| 11% (AAGL) | 16% | 5 pp | 487 | 974 |
| 11% (AAGL) | 18% | 7 pp | 260 | 520 |
| 15% (projected) | 20% | 5 pp | 523 | 1,046 |
| 15% (projected) | 22% | 7 pp | 279 | 558 |

**AAGL alone is underpowered for subgroup analyses.** With ~90 abstracts/year × 11 years = ~990 total abstracts and 122 definite matches, AAGL alone cannot support two-proportion tests within subgroups (e.g., by year, by session type, by research category). Splitting 122 events into 4 research categories yields ~30 events per cell, providing approximately 32% power to detect a 7 pp difference — substantially below the 80% threshold recommended for confirmatory analyses.

**Combined AAGL + SMFM + SGO reaches adequate power within 3 years.** AAGL + SMFM + SGO contribute approximately 90 + 500 + 570 = 1,160 abstracts/year. At an assumed 14% pooled publication rate, this generates approximately 162 new confirmed publications per year. Within 3 years of combined data collection, the pooled dataset would contain ~3,480 abstracts and ~487 confirmed publications — sufficient for two-proportion z-tests between any two of the three societies at 80% power for a 5 pp difference, and for Cox model subgroup analyses with adequate event counts (>50 per stratum) in all major subgroups (RCT vs. non-RCT, positive vs. negative results, US vs. international).

**Cox proportional hazards model power: event-count requirements.** For Cox model covariate effects, the rule of thumb is ≥10 events per predictor variable (EPP), with a minimum of 50 total events for stable hazard ratio estimation. The AAGL dataset with 122 definite matches over 11 years provides adequate power (EPP ≥ 10) for a model with up to 12 covariates — which matches the current model specification. However, for interaction terms (e.g., subspecialty × result positivity) at least 50 events per interaction cell are needed. AAGL alone (122 events total) cannot support such interactions. A combined 3-society dataset with ~487 events over 3 years would support Cox models with up to 6 interaction terms while maintaining EPP ≥ 10, enabling the first adequately powered cross-subspecialty time-to-publication analysis in OB/GYN.

### A11.7 Disambiguation Challenges by Society

Each society presents distinct challenges for the name disambiguation, gender inference, and identity resolution components of the pipeline. Understanding these challenges prospectively allows targeted solutions to be built into the ingestion layer rather than discovered post-hoc.

**AAGL.** The primary and most consequential challenge is initials-only first names (e.g., "R.S. Guido"). This single feature limits ORCID linkage to 15.8%, disables exact-name NPI matching for 84% of US authors, and reduces genderize.io inference accuracy. No other society in the candidate set has this limitation. The recommended mitigation — cross-referencing confirmed publications to retrieve full author names from PubMed XML — recovers full names for confirmed matches but leaves unmatched abstracts with initials only.

**SMFM.** The MFM community has a higher proportion of international presenters than MIGS (reflecting MFM's broader geographic scope in high-risk obstetrics). Authors with Hebrew, Arabic, Chinese, Korean, and South Asian names present two challenges: (1) genderize.io has lower accuracy for non-Western names (reported coverage 60–75% vs. >90% for Western European names); and (2) multi-character transliteration variants (e.g., "Weissman" vs. "Weisman") increase false-negative rates in PubMed author-name matching. The recommended mitigation is applying Unicode normalization before string matching and using ORCID-linked affiliations to anchor transliteration variants.

**SGO.** Gynecologic oncology fellows are disproportionately first authors at SGO, reflecting the mentorship structure of oncology training. Fellows who present in year 1 of fellowship may be attending physicians by year 3 (when the abstract most likely converts to a publication), meaning their NPI affiliation state changes between abstract submission and paper publication. This creates a temporal mismatch in state-level career-stage analyses. The mitigation is recording the abstract-submission year as the reference year for career-stage determination, not the publication year.

**ASRM.** ASRM abstracts include presentations by embryologists, andrologists, reproductive biologists, and PhD-level researchers who do not hold MD degrees and therefore do not appear in the ABOG NPI pool. An estimated 20–30% of ASRM first authors may be non-physicians, for whom the NPI matching component is not applicable. Affiliation text parsing (detecting "PhD," "HCLD," "MS" in credentials, or "laboratory" in institutional title) should flag non-physician authors before NPI matching to avoid spurious low-confidence matches.

**ACOG.** ACOG is the largest and most heterogeneous society, encompassing community general obstetrician-gynecologists, academic specialists, and residents presenting case reports. General OB/GYN practitioners are harder to NPI-match than subspecialists because (a) they outnumber subspecialists in the ABOG pool by approximately 3:1, increasing name collision probability, and (b) they are less likely to have institutional affiliation strings that disambiguate between similarly-named physicians in the same specialty. The MIGS subspecialty bonus used in the AAGL NPI scoring model should be disabled for ACOG and replaced with a generalist-aware prior.

**NASPAG.** The pediatric and adolescent gynecology community is small (~1,200 members). Many authors present at multiple annual meetings over their careers, creating an unusual opportunity for longitudinal author-level analysis within a single society. With only ~35 abstracts/year, repeat-presenter rates are high enough that a dedicated author-deduplication step (merging the same individual's presentations across years) would be scientifically valuable and technically feasible. This is not implemented in the current AAGL pipeline, where repeat presenters are treated independently.

**SFP.** The family planning community includes a notable proportion of non-OB/GYN investigators: epidemiologists, public health researchers, social scientists, and family medicine physicians who present on contraception access, abortion policy, and reproductive health disparities. These authors do not hold OB/GYN board certification and will not appear in the ABOG NPI pool. Additionally, SFP publications may appear in *Perspectives on Sexual and Reproductive Health*, *Contraception*, *American Journal of Public Health*, and other non-OB/GYN journals not captured by the 12-journal relevance scoring list in the current pipeline. Expanding the journal relevance list is recommended before applying the pipeline to SFP.

### A11.8 Recommended Implementation Sequence

The following phased schedule balances scientific priority, engineering complexity, and data volume to ensure each new society's data can be validated against AAGL results before committing to the next expansion.

**Phase 1 (Months 1–3): SMFM.** SMFM is the highest-priority first expansion because it shares the ScienceDirect platform with AAGL (zero new platform engineering), provides the strongest scientific contrast (maternal-fetal medicine vs. minimally invasive gynecologic surgery), and contributes the largest additional abstract volume of any single-platform society (~500/year). Full first names and affiliations are available at ingestion. The AJOG supplement URL pattern is confirmed across 2013–2023. The primary implementation tasks are: update journal DOI prefix and URL pattern in `01b_parse_web.R`; adjust NPI subspecialty filter to MFM; update journal relevance list to include AJOG, *Prenatal Diagnosis*, and *Ultrasound in Obstetrics & Gynecology*; validate against a 50-abstract manually verified gold standard.

**Phase 2 (Months 4–6): SGO.** SGO adds the gynecologic oncology perspective with ~570 abstracts/year on ScienceDirect. Implementation is identical to SMFM except for the 2013 data gap (no dedicated supplement in *Gynecologic Oncology* Vol 129) and the need to expand the journal relevance list to oncology-specific outlets (*Cancer*, *Journal of Clinical Oncology*, *Gynecologic Oncology*). The fellow-as-first-author challenge (described in A11.7) should be addressed by adding a career-stage temporal mismatch flag to the output CSV.

**Phase 3 (Months 7–9): NASPAG.** NASPAG completes the Elsevier/ScienceDirect implementation set at low volume (~35 abstracts/year). The small annual N means the full 2013–2023 dataset contains only ~385 abstracts — smaller than a single ASRM year — but the complete dataset can be processed in a single pipeline run. The longitudinal author-deduplication enhancement described in A11.7 should be implemented here, as NASPAG's small, stable community makes it the ideal test case.

**Phase 4 (Months 10–12): ASRM.** ASRM's institutional authentication requirement for supplement browsing is the primary implementation obstacle. The recommended approach is to enumerate abstract DOIs from the CrossRef API (which does not require authentication: `https://api.crossref.org/works?filter=issn:0015-0282,type:journal-article&query={year}`) and resolve individual DOIs via `https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fertnstert.{YYYY}.{MM}.{NNN}`, which resolves publicly even when the browse index requires auth. A non-physician-author detection flag should be added to the output schema before ASRM processing. With ~1,200 abstracts/year across 11 years, ASRM is the most computationally intensive society and should be run on a multi-core machine with `furrr::future_map()` parallelization.

**Phase 5 (Year 2): LWW Societies — ACOG, CREOG/APGO, AUGS.** These three societies share the LWW platform and can be processed once a single LWW scraper is built. The scraper should handle: JavaScript-rendered TOC pagination (use `httr2` with the LWW API endpoint or `RSelenium` as fallback), LWW-specific HTML affiliation extraction (plain-text rather than JSON-blob), and hash-based DOI format (`10.1097/01.AOG.XXXXXXXX`). Building and validating the scraper against the AUGS 2023 supplement (the most recent and structurally representative) is recommended before applying it to ACOG and CREOG/APGO. The three societies can then be processed in parallel.

**Defer: SFP and SASGOG.** SFP's collection-level DOI model requires parsing full-text collection articles — a fundamentally different ingestion approach that should be tackled only after the volume societies (SMFM, SGO, ASRM) are validated. SASGOG lacks a journal, lacks DOIs, and lacks PubMed indexing; implementing the pipeline for SASGOG provides no DOI-chain search benefit and requires custom web scraping of a society website with unknown historical URL structure. SASGOG is deferred indefinitely unless the society introduces journal publication.

### A11.9 Scientific Questions Enabled by Multi-Society Data

A fully assembled 10-society OB/GYN abstract-to-publication dataset would enable the following research questions, listed with expected effect sizes where empirical estimates exist in the literature and with the covariate structure available in the combined dataset.

**Hypothesis 1: Cross-subspecialty publication rate heterogeneity.** Publication rates at surgical subspecialty meetings (AAGL, AUGS, SGO) differ from those at medical/consultative subspecialty meetings (SMFM, ASRM) after controlling for study design and year. *Expected effect size:* Based on single-society studies, surgical meeting rates range 15–30% (SGO: Salani et al. 2008, 29%; AAGL current: 11.4%) and medical meeting rates range 20–40% (SMFM: Dunbar et al. 2022). A 10–15 pp difference between surgical and medical meetings is plausible. *Available covariates:* society, subspecialty, year, RCT status, result positivity, first-author gender, career stage, US vs. international affiliation.

**Hypothesis 2: The AAGL initials-only effect.** Controlling for all other factors, AAGL abstracts will show lower confirmed publication rates than structurally similar Elsevier societies (SMFM, SGO, NASPAG) — not because MIGS researchers publish less, but because the pipeline's name-based search is less effective with initials. This methodological artifact can be estimated directly by comparing AAGL match rates for abstracts with confirmed publications (where full names can be recovered from PubMed) vs. abstract-only records. *Expected magnitude:* 2–5 pp rate undercount attributable to initials-only limitation.

**Hypothesis 3: Publication bias is consistent across subspecialties.** The positive result publication advantage observed in AAGL (HR for positive results in Cox model: direction and magnitude to be confirmed from the current analysis) is replicated across societies. If the hazard ratio for positive-result abstracts is consistent across subspecialties (I² < 25% in random-effects meta-analysis across societies), this supports a domain-general publication bias mechanism rather than subspecialty-specific editorial norms. *Covariate:* `result_positivity` (already extracted by NLP in the current pipeline).

**Hypothesis 4: Gender parity trends differ by subspecialty.** SMFM and NASPAG — the two OB/GYN subspecialties with the highest proportion of women practitioners — will show earlier gender parity in first authorship than AAGL (historically male-dominated MIGS) or SGO (historically male-dominated surgical oncology). *Available covariates:* `gender_unified` (genderize.io), `congress_year`, society. *Expected pattern based on prior literature:* 2–4% annual increase in female first-authorship in surgical subspecialties (Rao et al. 2021); parity already achieved in SMFM by 2015 (Bhatt et al. 2019 reported 55% female at SMFM meetings).

**Hypothesis 5: RCT design advantage is attenuated in educational research.** The AAGL Cox model shows a hazard ratio of 2.36 for RCT vs. non-RCT abstracts — a strong design advantage for the gold-standard study type. In CREOG/APGO data, where the majority of presentations are simulation studies and curriculum evaluations, the RCT is less culturally normative. The RCT hazard ratio in CREOG/APGO data is expected to be smaller (projected HR 1.4–1.8) or non-significant, reflecting the different publication norms of medical education research.

**Combined dataset covariate matrix.** A fully assembled multi-society dataset would contain the following covariates for each abstract: `society` (10-level factor), `platform` (ScienceDirect/LWW/web), `doi_type` (individual/collection/none), `congress_year` (2012–2023), `n_abstracts_that_year` (society-year abstract volume), `first_author_full_name_available` (logical), `result_positivity`, `is_rct`, `study_design`, `research_category`, `gender_unified`, `first_author_country`, `first_author_career_stage`, `practice_type`, `subspecialty_npi`, `has_industry_funding`, `has_trial_registration`, and all matching-quality flags (`classification`, `best_score`, `n_candidates`, `has_tie`). This covariate richness supports multi-level modeling with society as the clustering unit and abstract-year as the within-cluster observation.

### A11.10 SMFM Abstract Supplement URLs (2013–2023)

SMFM (Society for Maternal-Fetal Medicine) annual meeting abstracts are published as supplement issues of the *American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology* (AJOG) on ScienceDirect (Elsevier). Unlike AUGS abstracts, **each individual SMFM abstract receives a unique DOI** following the pattern `10.1016/j.ajog.YYYY.MM.NNN`, where NNN increments sequentially within the supplement. This was confirmed by inspecting the 2023 supplement, where abstracts are numbered sequentially (…004, …005, …006, etc.).

This is the same DOI structure as AAGL/JMIG abstracts, meaning the full pipeline — including DOI-chain search via OpenAlex, CrossRef metadata retrieval, and ScienceDirect HTML affiliation backfill — can be applied without modification.

**Note:** The 2021 supplement appears in Issue 2 rather than Issue 1, likely due to meeting rescheduling from the COVID-19 virtual format. All other years use Issue 1.

| Year | Meeting | AJOG Vol | Issue | ScienceDirect URL | DOI Pattern |
|------|---------|----------|-------|-------------------|-------------|
| 2023 | 43rd | 228 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/228/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2022.11.xxx |
| 2022 | 42nd | 226 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/226/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2021.11.xxx |
| 2021 | 41st | 224 | **2**, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/224/issue/2/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2020.12.xxx |
| 2020 | 40th | 222 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/222/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2019.11.xxx |
| 2019 | 39th | 220 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/220/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2018.11.xxx |
| 2018 | 38th | 218 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/218/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2017.11.xxx |
| 2017 | 37th | 216 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/216/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2016.11.xxx |
| 2016 | 36th | 214 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/214/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2015.11.xxx |
| 2015 | 35th | 212 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/212/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2014.11.xxx |
| 2014 | 34th | 210 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/210/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2013.11.xxx |
| 2013 | 33rd | 208 | 1, Suppl S | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/american-journal-of-obstetrics-and-gynecology/vol/208/issue/1/suppl/S | 10.1016/j.ajog.2012.11.xxx |

**DOI uniqueness confirmed:** Individual abstract DOIs increment sequentially within each supplement. Because AJOG is hosted on ScienceDirect (Elsevier) — the same platform as JMIG — the existing `backfill_affiliations_from_cache.R`, `01b_parse_web.R`, and `03c_doi_chain_search.R` scripts can be adapted for SMFM with minimal modification, primarily updating the journal-specific URL patterns and PII extraction logic.

*Sources: ScienceDirect AJOG special issues index; https://publications.smfm.org/*

### A11.11 ASRM Abstract Supplement URLs (2013–2023)

ASRM (American Society for Reproductive Medicine) annual meeting abstracts are published as supplement issues of *Fertility and Sterility* (ISSN 0015-0282) on ScienceDirect (Elsevier). **Every abstract receives a unique DOI** in the format `10.1016/j.fertnstert.YYYY.MM.NNN`, where NNN is a sequential article number. DOI uniqueness is guaranteed by the DOI standard, enforced by CrossRef at ingestion (duplicates are rejected), and confirmed by spot-check of 50 sampled DOIs across years.

Because *Fertility and Sterility* is hosted on ScienceDirect (Elsevier) — the same platform as JMIG and AJOG — the existing pipeline components for DOI-chain search, ScienceDirect HTML affiliation extraction, and web scraping apply directly.

**Note:** Full supplement browsing requires institutional ScienceDirect authentication (fertstert.org and sciencedirect.com return 403 for unauthenticated access). Individual abstracts resolve publicly via `https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fertnstert.YYYY.MM.NNN`. PubMed indexes selected abstracts from these supplements and is freely accessible.

| Year | ASRM Meeting | Volume | Issue | DOI Pattern |
|------|-------------|--------|-------|-------------|
| 2023 | 79th Annual | 120 | 4 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2023.MM.xxx |
| 2022 | 78th Annual | 118 | 4 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2022.MM.xxx |
| 2021 | 77th Annual | 116 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2021.MM.xxx |
| 2020 | 76th Annual | 114 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2020.MM.xxx |
| 2019 | 75th Annual | 112 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2019.MM.xxx |
| 2018 | 74th Annual | 110 | 4 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2018.MM.xxx |
| 2017 | 73rd Annual | 108 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2017.MM.xxx |
| 2016 | 72nd Annual | 106 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2016.MM.xxx |
| 2015 | 71st Annual | 104 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2015.MM.xxx |
| 2014 | 70th Annual | 102 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2014.MM.xxx |
| 2013 | 69th Annual | 100 | 3 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2013.MM.xxx |

**Abstract counts per year (CrossRef):**

| Year | DOIs indexed in CrossRef |
|------|--------------------------|
| 2023 | 940 |
| 2022 | 1,142 |
| 2021 | 1,239 |
| 2020 | 1,456 |
| 2019 | 1,259 |
| 2018 | 1,214 |
| 2017 | 1,111 |
| 2016 | 1,147 |
| 2015 | 1,153 |
| 2014 | 1,227 |
| 2013 | 2,010 |

**Advance program DOIs** (publicly resolvable "Scientific Papers to Be Presented" TOC documents):

| Year | Advance Program DOI |
|------|---------------------|
| 2013 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2013.01.001 |
| 2014 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2013.11.036 |
| 2015 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2014.12.002 |
| 2016 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2015.12.123 |
| 2017 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2017.02.003 |
| 2018 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2018.02.014 |
| 2019 | 10.1016/j.fertnstert.2019.02.032 |
| 2022 | 10.1016/s0015-0282(22)01984-7 |

*Note: Advance program DOIs for 2020, 2021, and 2023 were not recoverable from CrossRef search.*

**Comparison with AAGL and SMFM for pipeline reuse:**

| Feature | AAGL (JMIG) | SMFM (AJOG) | ASRM (F&S) | AUGS (Urogynecology) |
|---------|-------------|-------------|------------|----------------------|
| Individual abstract DOIs | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Elsevier/ScienceDirect | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (LWW) |
| DOI-chain search applicable | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Affiliation backfill applicable | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Auth required to browse | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| CrossRef abstract count (avg/year) | ~90 | ~500 | ~1,300 | ~400 |

ASRM is the largest of the four societies by abstract volume (~1,100–2,000/year vs. ~90 for AAGL), making it the highest-powered dataset for publication-rate analysis but also the most computationally intensive to process.

### A11.12 Society of Family Planning (SFP) Abstract URLs (2013–2023)

SFP (Society of Family Planning) annual meeting abstracts are published in *Contraception* (ISSN 0010-7824, Elsevier) as one or two regular journal articles — not as a supplement issue. This is a structurally different publication model from SMFM and ASRM:

- **Abstract collection article:** All submitted abstracts published as a single article with one DOI
- **Featured research article:** Selected high-scoring abstracts published as a companion article with a separate DOI (introduced ~2018)
- **Individual abstracts do NOT have unique DOIs** — the entire collection shares one DOI per year

The meeting was called the **North American Forum on Family Planning (NAFP)** through 2018, then renamed to the **SFP Annual Meeting** starting 2019. Abstract collections for 2013 and 2014 could not be located in PubMed or ScienceDirect; these years may not have been published in *Contraception* or may have appeared in a different format. A 2017 "TEMPORARY REMOVAL" notice on PubMed may indicate a correction or republication.

| Year | Meeting | Abstract Collection DOI | Featured Research DOI | PubMed |
|------|---------|------------------------|----------------------|--------|
| 2023 | SFP Annual | 10.1016/j.contraception.2023.110145 | 10.1016/j.contraception.2023.110144 | https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37821000/ |
